equation: $\begin{array} { r } { \ln \left( \frac { \mathrm { H T } } { { T _ { \mathrm { x } } } ^ { 2 } } \right) = - \frac { E } { R T _ { \mathrm { x } } } + } \end{array}$
description: The equation presented is the Kissinger relation, which describes the dependence of the onset temperature of a thermally activated process on the applied heating rate. In this expression, \( \mathrm{HT} \) denotes the heating rate, \( T_{\mathrm{x}} \) represents the onset temperature at which the process (such as grain coarsening) is observed, \( E \) is the apparent activation energy for the process, and \( R \) is the universal gas constant. The equation captures a linear relationship between the logarithmic term \( \ln(\mathrm{HT}/T_{\mathrm{x}}^{2}) \) and the inverse of the onset temperature \( 1/T_{\mathrm{x}} \), where the slope is proportional to \(-E/R\). This formulation allows for the determination of the activation energy from experimental measurements of how the onset temperature shifts with varying heating rates.

The equation \(T_{\mathrm{x}} = 393\ \mathrm{K}\) defines the onset temperature for grain coarsening, denoted by \(T_{\mathrm{x}}\), as 393 kelvin. This specific numerical value corresponds to the experimentally determined temperature at which the onset of grain boundary migration occurs in a material with an initial grain size of 80 nm under a constant heating rate of 1 K/min. Within the framework of the Kissinger analysis for thermally activated processes, this onset temperature is a critical parameter that links the kinetics of microstructural evolution to the applied thermal schedule.

The equation \(T_{\mathrm{x}} = 473 \, \mathrm{K}\) defines the specific onset temperature for a microstructural transition. Here, \(T_{\mathrm{x}}\) denotes the onset temperature for grain boundary migration, which is the point at which significant grain coarsening begins in a nanocrystalline material under a constant heating rate. The value of 473 K corresponds to the calculated temperature at a heating rate of 62 K/min, based on the Kissinger relationship. This temperature is critical because it exceeds the characteristic temperature for the formation of annealing twins in copper, suggesting that under sufficiently rapid heating, twin formation can precede and potentially trigger grain boundary relaxation before coarsening occurs.

The equation provides a method for estimating the twin probability, denoted by \(\beta\), from X-ray diffraction peak asymmetry measurements. Here, \(\Delta.C.G.(2\theta_{hkl}^{\circ})\) represents the difference in the center of gravity for the diffraction peak corresponding to the Miller indices \((hkl)\), measured at the Bragg angle \(2\theta_{hkl}\). The specific peaks used are the \((111)\) and \((200)\) reflections. The numerator quantifies the differential peak shift between these two reflections, which is sensitive to twinning. The denominator, \(11\tan\theta_{111} + 14.6\tan\theta_{200}\), acts as a scaling factor that incorporates the geometric dependence on the Bragg angles \(\theta_{111}\) and \(\theta_{200}\). Thus, the expression relates the measurable asymmetry in peak positions to the probability of twinning within the crystal structure.
